coredata.dc:title: Nonenzymatic detection of glucose based on Cu2+ catalytic oxidation on N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guanhong
Li, Xinyi
Yu, Zhiyue
Ren, Peipei
Yang, Fan
Wang, Jun
Song, Youtao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a new fluorescent strategy for detecting glucose based on copper ions supported on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to form Cu2+/N-CQDs nanocomplexes. The N-CQDs were characterized using high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The optical properties of the N-CQDs were also determined by ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The proposed strategy comprises three steps: quenching the fluorescence from the N-CQDs with Cu2+ ions, producing Cu2O and gluconic acid via the Cu-catalyzed oxidation of glucose, and leaving Cu2+ ions on the surfaces of the N-CQDs to recover the fluorescence intensity. In the Cu2+/N-CQDs nanocomplexes, the Cu2+ ions act as quenchers to decrease the fluorescence from N-CQDs by surface energy transfer. The Cu-catalyzed oxidation of glucose reduces the Cu2+ ions to form Cu2O, which frees the Cu2+ ions from the surfaces of the N-CQDs and restores the fluorescence of the N-CQDs. Therefore, the change in the fluorescence depends on the concentrations of Cu2+ and glucose. Under optimal conditions, the quantitative analysis of glucose had a detection limit of 0.455 μM. Thus, Cu2+/N-CQDs can be applied as nonenzymatic glucose probes to detect low concentrations of glucose. This method also has advantages due to the facile preparation of the nanomaterials, as well as high analytical sensitivity and low experimental costs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Cu-catalyzed glucose oxidation
Nonenzymatic glucose detection
Glucose fluorescence analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS analysis of N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs
UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra obtained for Cu2+/N-CQDs
Effects of Cu2+ ion concentration, gluconic acid, and interferents
Principle and mechanism of glucose detection using Cu2+/N-CQDs as fluorescent probes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: China National Critical Project for Science and Technology on Water Pollution Prevention and Control

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Science Foundation of China ( 21371084 , 31570154 ) and Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China ( 2015ZX07202-012 ). We also thank our colleagues and other students for participating in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (a-1: low-resolution and a-2: high-resolution), size distribution histogram (b), XRD spectrum (c), FT-IR spectrum (d), and XPS patterns (e) obtained for N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) (λex = 310–390 nm) obtained for N-CQDs in aqueous solution.
Effects of solution acidity (pH) (a) and temperature (°C) (b) on the fluorescence spectra (λex = 345 nm) obtained for N-CQDs in aqueous solution.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra obtained for N-CQDs, N-CQDs + Cu2+, and CuSO4 solutions. (b) Images of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + Cu2+ in aqueous solutions under daylight (b-1) and ultraviolet light (365 nm) (b-2). (c) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 345 nm) obtained for N-CQDs in aqueous solutions with different Cu2+ ion concentrations from 0 to 2000 μM. (d) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity (λem = 450 nm) of N-CQDs and the concentration of Cu2+ ions.

Effects of solution acidity (pH) (a-1) and glucose concentrations (b-1) ranging from 0 mM to 2.0 mM on the fluorescent spectra (λex = 345 nm) obtained for Cu2+/N-CQDs solutions in the presence of glucose, as well as the corresponding fluorescent histogram comparison (λem = 450 nm) (a-2) and the calibration curve for the fluorescence intensities (λem = 450 nm) (b-2).

Effects of high Cu2+ ion concentrations (more than 1000 μM) (a) and gluconic acid concentrations (0–2.00 mM) (b) on glucose detection in the presence of the N-CQDs as fluorescent probes (λex = 345 nm).

(a) Effects of interferents (2.00 mM) on glucose detection using N-CQDs as fluorescent probes (λex = 345 nm) in the presence of Cu2+ ions, and (b) the corresponding fluorescent histogram comparison (λem = 450 nm).
Mechanism of glucose detection using N-CQDs as fluorescent probes in the presence of Cu2+ ions.
Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs).
Comparison of experimental results obtained using different glucose detection methods.

NiO HHs-Nafion/GCE
C-dots/AgNPs
MWCNT/NiCo/GCE PAA-rGO/VS-PANI/LuPc2/GOx-MFH
Ni@f-MWCNT/GCE
CuO/r-GO Electrode
N-CQDs

Nee
Ef
Nee
Ef
Nee
Nee
Nef

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NiO HHs-Nafion/GCE: Hedgehog-like NiO nanostructures modified on glassy carbon electrode; C-dots/AgNPs: carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles; MWCNT/NiCo/GCE: Ni/NiCo nanowire filled multiwalled carbon nanotubes on a glassy carbon electrode; PAA-rGO/VS-PANI/LuPc2/GOx-MFH: phthalocyanine (LuPc2) as a three-dimensional robust matrix for glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilization; Ni@f-MWCNT/GCE: nickel nanoparticles modified on a glassy carbon electrode; CuO/r-GO Electrode: CuO/reduced graphene oxide modified electrodes; Cu2+/N-CQDs: Cu2+ supported on N-doped carbon quantum dots.
Nee: nonenzymatic electrochemistry method; Ef: enzymatic fluorescence method; Nef: nonenzymatic fluorescence method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Glucose detection in human urine samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Nonenzymatic detection of glucose based on Cu2+ catalytic oxidation on N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a new fluorescent strategy for detecting glucose based on copper ions supported on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to form Cu2+/N-CQDs nanocomplexes. The N-CQDs were characterized using high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The optical properties of the N-CQDs were also determined by ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The proposed strategy comprises three steps: quenching the fluorescence from the N-CQDs with Cu2+ ions, producing Cu2O and gluconic acid via the Cu-catalyzed oxidation of glucose, and leaving Cu2+ ions on the surfaces of the N-CQDs to recover the fluorescence intensity. In the Cu2+/N-CQDs nanocomplexes, the Cu2+ ions act as quenchers to decrease the fluorescence from N-CQDs by surface energy transfer. The Cu-catalyzed oxidation of glucose reduces the Cu2+ ions to form Cu2O, which frees the Cu2+ ions from the surfaces of the N-CQDs and restores the fluorescence of the N-CQDs. Therefore, the change in the fluorescence depends on the concentrations of Cu2+ and glucose. Under optimal conditions, the quantitative analysis of glucose had a detection limit of 0.455 μM. Thus, Cu2+/N-CQDs can be applied as nonenzymatic glucose probes to detect low concentrations of glucose. This method also has advantages due to the facile preparation of the nanomaterials, as well as high analytical sensitivity and low experimental costs.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past 10 years, the number of people with type 2 diabetes has increased alongside the rise in obesity in developed and developing countries [1]. Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin secretion defects or dysfunction. Persistent hyperglycemia and chronic metabolic disturbances can injure all tissues and organs [2–4]. In particular, they can cause damage to the eyes, kidneys, cardiovascular system, and nervous system, as well as dysfunction and failure. Severely affected patients can present with dehydration, electrolyte disorders, and acid-base disorders [5,6]. Glucose is one of the most significant metabolic materials and it plays many crucial roles in human physiological processes. An abnormal level of glucose is a source of multiple medical disorders [7–9]. Thus, monitoring and controlling glucose at a strict management level is extremely important.

In general, two main methods are employed for detecting glucose: enzymatic and nonenzymatic methods [10–12]. The enzymatic method for glucose detection is fast and accurate, but it is problematic because the enzyme employed is a protein, and thus its activity and stability are highly dependent on the environmental conditions [13–16]. In contrast to the enzymatic method, the nonenzymatic method for glucose detection is beneficial because it is simple to operate, cost-effective, and stable in the long term [17–19]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of fluorescent materials in the analytic chemistry field with many superior features, including simple preparation and functionalization, low toxicity, and high stability [20–22]. In fact, similar carbon nanomaterials have been combined with electrochemical methods for the detection of glucose. However, agglomeration, weak electrical conductivity, and the mechanical instability of carbon nanomaterials hinder the widespread application of nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensors [8,11,18].

It is well known that the aldehyde group in glucose is susceptible to oxidization. In the presence of copper ions, which are typically used as catalysts, the oxidation reaction can be performed almost completely for glucose under alkaline conditions [22,23]. In addition, glucose can be identified based on the “bronze mirror reaction.” However, this detection method is difficult to apply directly to the small amounts of glucose present in the human body. Hence, CQDs could be employed for the detection of glucose due to their superior optical and chemical properties, where detection can be achieved based on changes in fluorescence even with a very tiny amount of glucose. The most important feature of CQDs is that they are not sensitive to disruption by external factors and they are highly tolerant of different conditions [27,28]. In this study, we developed a strategy for glucose detection based on some special properties of N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). First, Cu2+ ions act as “quenchers” to decrease the fluorescence of N-CQDs by surface energy transfer from N-CQDs to Cu2+ and Cu2+/N-CQDs complexes are formed simultaneously. Next, under weak alkaline conditions, the Cu2+/N-CQDs can combine with OH− to form an N-CQDs-Cu(OH) complex, which catalyzes the oxidation reaction for glucose in a similar manner to Cu(OH)2. Subsequently, the glucose is oxidized to gluconic acid without requiring glucose oxidase and Cu2O is formed due to the reduction of Cu2+. Apparently, the production of gluconic acid is catalyzed by the Cu2+/N-CQDs via the oxidation reaction of glucose in a weak alkaline solution. Finally, the Cu2+ ions break away from the surface of the N-CQDs and the fluorescence by the N-CQDs recovers.

In this study, we prepared N-CQDs using citric acid and urea via a one-step microwave hydrothermal synthesis method without any passivation reagents. The N-CQDs exhibited high optical activity and chemical stability. In addition, the N-CQDs exhibited an interesting “ON-OFF-ON” mode with three different state emissions due to the orderly addition of Cu2+ ions and glucose. Cu2+ ions were combined with N-CQDs to form Cu2+/N-CQDs complexes, which were used as nonenzymatic probes in the catalytic oxidation of glucose. The detection system was not affected by interference from biomolecules and other inorganic ions in biological body fluids. Therefore, Cu2+/N-CQDs can be used in an economical nonenzymatic glucose detection method as an effective fluorescence probe.

A microwave hydrothermal synthesis instrument (XH-800G, Xianghu Science and Technology Ltd, China) was used to prepare the N-CQDs. The size and morphology of the N-CQDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TECNAI F20, Microscopy and Analytical Imaging Laboratory, USA), which operated at 200 kV. Samples were prepared by dropping a water dispersion onto carbon-coated copper TEM grids using pipettes and dried under ambient condition. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker-AXS D-8, Germany) with Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range from 10° to 70°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using an FT-IR spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 5700, Nicolet Company, USA) with the KBr pellet technique over the range from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a photoelectron instrument (Thermo ESCALAB 250, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) with Al Kα radiation. The binding energy was calibrated against the carbon 1s line. All fluorescence measurements were obtained using a fluorophotometer (Cary 300, Varian Company, USA) with the excitation slit set at the 10 nm band pass and emission at the 5 nm band pass in a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were acquired with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 50, Varian Company, USA). Light at 365 nm from a UV lamp was used to irradiate the N-CQDs in solution. A global digital pH meter (FE20K, Mettler-Toledo Company, Switzerland) was used to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions.

Citric acid and urea were obtained from Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant (China) to prepare the N-CQDs. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, pure analytical reagent), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, pure analytical reagent), and sodium chloride (NaCl, pure analytical reagent) (Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant, China) were used to adjust the pH and salt concentration in the solutions. A standard Cu2+ ion stock solution (5000 μmol/L) was prepared by dissolving CuSO4 (pure analytical reagent, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China) in ultrapure water. Other biomolecules used as interference substances (pure analytical reagent, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China) were prepared by dissolving in ultrapure water. All of the reagents and chemicals were analytical reagent grade. Aqueous solutions were all prepared with ultrapure water produced using a water purifier nanopure water system (18.3 MΩ/cm, Hitech-Sciencetool, Shanghai, China).

The N-CQDs were prepared according to the process shown in Scheme 1 . Citric acid and urea were mixed in ultrapure water and subjected to one-step microwave hydrothermal synthesis [32]. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at a constant temperature of 200 °C for 2.00 h, before cooling to room temperature. After filtration, the filter liquor was collected from the mixture using a pipette and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The remaining brown transparent concentrated solution was dialyzed in ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for 24 h, where the ultrapure water was changed every 8.0 h. The N-CQDs solutions (0.758 mg/mL) obtained were collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4.0 °C.

The N-CQDs were combined with Cu2+ ions in an ultrapure water solution. In a typical assay, 2.00 mL of the N-CQDs in aqueous solution was added to a quartz cuvette. An appropriate volume of Cu2+ ions was diluted from the working solution to obtain the required concentration with ultrapure water and added to the N-CQDs solution described above. The mixed Cu2+/N-CQDs solution was used directly without further dialysis. The solutions containing Cu2+ ions (1000 μM) were mixed thoroughly by vortexing. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. All of the experiments were performed at room temperature.

In a typical experiment, 3.00 mL of Cu2+/N-CQDs and 3.00 mL of glucose at various concentrations were incubated in test tubes. The pH of each mixture was adjusted to 7.50. The test tubes were placed in a water bath and maintained at a temperature of 80 °C for about 15 min, before cooling to room temperature and recording the fluorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm in order to evaluate the glucose detection performance of the Cu2+/N-CQDs. In addition, the selectivity of the N-CQDs for glucose detection was confirmed by adding other solutions containing interferents (2.00 mM) instead of glucose, which were analyzed in the same manner described above.

Urine samples were obtained from volunteers at Liaoning University. Before their analysis, the urine samples were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min and passed through an ultrafilter with a molecular weight cut off of 3000 Da. Next, the urine samples were diluted and different concentrations of glucose (0 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, and 1.0 mM) were added. Finally, the prepared samples were used for detecting glucose according to the procedure described above. The fluorescence spectrum was then recorded using excitation at a wavelength of 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS analysis of N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 (a-1), the characteristics determined by TEM demonstrated that the N-CQDs were obtained successfully. The N-CQDs comprised uniformly dispersed spherical dots with an average diameter of 1.4 nm. The high-resolution TEM image in Figs. 1(a-2) shows the highly micro-crystalline features of the prepared N-CQDs with a crystalline plane spacing of 0.110 nm. The crystalline plane spacing was not particularly clear in the high-resolution image, possibly because the N-CQDs were very small, with even smaller lattice spacing. In addition, the TEM image of the Cu2+/N-CQDs in Fig. S1 demonstrates that there were no obvious differences compared with the N-CQDs, which may be attributed to the good adsorption of Cu2+ ions on the surfaces of the N-CQDs. Due to the extremely small size of Cu2+ ions, they could not be observed in the TEM image of the Cu2+/N-CQDs. Approximately 100 particles were randomly selected to measure the particle size and the calculated size distribution histogram was obtained. The distribution histogram for the particle sizes is shown in Fig. 1(b), which demonstrates that the average diameter was mainly distributed in the range of 1.2–1.4 nm. The XRD spectrum in Fig. 1(c) shows that there was only one diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23° for the N-CQDs, thereby indicating that the N-CQDs were highly disordered carbon atoms [33].

The compositions and the chemical configurations of the N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs were also analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1(d-1), the N-CQDs exhibited a broad peak at 3412 cm−1, which corresponded to the absorption of OH and NH stretching vibrations. In addition, a stretching vibration peak due to COC bonds appeared at 1050 cm−1. The presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups indicates that the N-CQDs had excellent solubility in water [34]. Methyl and methylene absorption peaks were detected in the prepared N-CQDs at 2851 cm −1 and 2920 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 2968 cm−1 and 1451 cm−1 could be assigned to the characteristic stretching vibrations and bending vibration of saturated hydrocarbons CH and CH2 , respectively [35,36]. In addition, the peak at 1649 nm−1 was related to the bending vibration of NH and the absorption peak at 1272 cm−1 was attributed to CN stretching vibrations, which are generally found in N-CQDs [37,38]. It should be noted that vibration absorption bands due to nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups were present in the FT-IR spectrum, and they could have introduced many defects to serve as excitation energy traps [39,40] and contributed to the enhanced luminescent capacity. In addition, the nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups present on the surface had a high affinity for Cu2+ ions, which was advantageous for the capture of Cu2+ ions. Figs. 1(d-2) demonstrates that the basic compositions and structures of the N-CQDs were unchanged in the Cu2+/N-CQDs. However, the peak intensities were slightly weaker because of the consumption of some groups. In particular, the Cu2+ ions formed coordination bonds with COOH, OH, and NH2 groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs. Therefore, the intensities of the original sharp peaks located at 1649 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, and 3412 cm−1 became weaker, thereby suggesting that the Cu2+ ions were coordinated with the COOH, OH, and NH2 groups associated with the N-CQDs.

The detailed elemental analyses of N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs based on XPS measurements are shown in Fig. 1(e−1) and Fig. 1(e−2), respectively. Fig. 1(e−1) shows that the N-CQDs produced three peaks at 285.08 eV, 399.73 eV, and 532.07 eV, which corresponded to C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s), respectively, and the atomic concentrations of these constituents were 59.38% (C), 11.71% (N), and 28.90% (O). The C (1s) spectrum (Fig. 1(e−1-C)) could be further divided into three peaks at 286.6 eV (sp3 C), 285.11 eV (CN), and 287.94 eV (CO) [41]. The N (1s) spectrum (Fig. 1(e−1-N)) indicated the presence of the NC=O (401.65 eV) peak in the N (1s) core level XPS spectra [42]. As shown in Figs. 1(e−2), the peaks for C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) in the Cu2+/N-CQDs were essentially the same as those in the N-CQDs. In addition, a new peak at 935.50 eV corresponded to Cu(2p). Figure 1(e−2-Cu) shows the presence of Cu (2p1/2) (935.05 eV) and Cu (2p3/2) (955.28 eV) peaks in the Cu (2p) core level XPS spectra. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrated that the N atoms derived from urea entered into the framework of the N-CQDs, and Cu2+ ions were associated with the N-CQDs on the surfaces of the Cu2+/N-CQDs, thereby confirming the results of the analyses based on FT-IR data.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained for N-CQDs in aqueous solution in order to determine their optical properties. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2 (a)) obtained for the N-CQDs in aqueous solution contained two absorption peaks at 242 nm and 347 nm, respectively. The absorption at 242 nm could be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain, thereby leading to fluorescence that was not visible to the naked eye [43]. The broad adsorption peak centered at 347 nm was due to the trapping of excited carriers by the localized surface states [44], which could generate a strong fluorescent emission.

The optical properties of the N-CQDs were also investigated based on their fluorescence spectra. We determined whether the N-CQDs produced emission peaks that depended on the excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence spectra obtained under light excitation at different wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 390 nm are shown in Fig. 2(b). Only the intensity of the fluorescence changed with the wavelength and the position of the peak did not shift in the emission spectra, thereby indicating that the fluorescent emission produced by the N-CQDs was virtually excitation-independent [45]. Fig. S2 shows that the intensity of the fluorescence increased gradually as the excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 345 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The intensity of the fluorescence then decreased as the excitation wavelength increased from 345 nm to 390 nm. Thus, in the following analysis of the fluorescence spectra, we used 345 nm as the optimum excitation wavelength.

Many carboxyls, hydroxyls, and other groups were present on the surfaces of the N-CQDs. However, the states of the surface groups might change in response to external factors, and resistance to environmental influences is important when using CQDs as fluorescent probes. Thus, we studied the effects of the solution pH and temperature on the fluorescence spectra produced by the N-CQDs in aqueous solution. The response in terms of the fluorescence by the N-CQDs to different pH values is shown in Fig. 3 (a), which indicates that the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs varied due to different electrostatic interactions [46,47]. Fig. S3(a-2) clearly demonstrates that the N-CQDs had low fluorescence intensities in an extremely acidic solution because the fluorescent emissions of the N-CQDs were quenched under strongly acidic conditions. The fluorescence intensity then increased gradually with the pH value. The N-CQDs in aqueous solution still has good luminance produced clear fluorescence even in strongly alkali conditions.

Furthermore, Fig. 3(b) indicates that the intensity of the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was affected little by the temperature. Fig. S3(b-2) clearly illustrates that the intensity of the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs solution was strongest at 30 °C. When the N-CQDs solutions were heated from 30 °C to 100 °C, they still maintained high fluorescence intensities and they were almost unaffected by the temperature. Therefore, these results clearly demonstrate that the N-CQDs potentially have broad application ranges.

The physiological (or clinical) concentrations of sodium chloride (NaCl) are usually fixed in the range of 0.85–0.90% in mammals (including humans) and at 0.67–0.70% for amphibians, in order to maintain normal osmotic pressure. Thus, we also investigated whether the NaCl concentration affected the fluorescence spectra produced by the N-CQDs in aqueous solution. The results showed that the intensity of the fluorescence produced under excitation at a wavelength of 345 nm remained almost unchanged, even at a high ionic strength of 1.00 mol/L (Fig. S3(c)). The emissive property of the N-CQDs was insensitive to the changes in ionic strength, thereby suggesting that the N-CQDs could be applied in biological detection. In addition, after storage for more than five months, the N-CQDs still produced almost identical emission spectra compared to the freshly prepared samples, which demonstrated the very high stability of the N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra obtained for Cu2+/N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the decay of fluorescence can provide information about the excited state for CQDs [48]. Electron transfer or a chemical change in the fluorophore induces fluorescence quenching. According to the UV–Vis spectra obtained for the N-CQDs in aqueous solution (Fig. 4 (a)), the absorption peak obtained for the Cu2+/N-CQDs around 347 nm was obviously lower than that for the N-CQDs. When the N-CQDs solution was mixed with Cu2+ ions, a large number of functional groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs bound with Cu2+ ions via ion exchange or electrostatic attraction. The available adsorption sites were increasingly occupied by Cu2+ ions, so the absorption peaks obtained for the N-CQDs weakened sharply. Nevertheless, the position of the absorption peak determined for the N-CQDs after adding Cu2+ ions was not displaced and it remained at the initial position found for the N-CQDs, thereby demonstrating that the addition of Cu2+ ions could quench the fluorescence by the N-CQDs, which was only induced by electron transfer. The chemical composition of the N-CQDs was unchanged after binding the Cu2+ ions, so we suggest that the amino groups associated with the N-CQDs acted as the dominant ligand atoms that coordinated with the Cu2+ ions. The Cu2+ ion had a strong affinity and distinctive selectivity for the N-CQDs due to the higher thermodynamic affinity and rapid integration process with N atoms on the surfaces of the N-CQDs [34]. Therefore, the NH2 groups present in the N-CQDs were likely to combine with the Cu2+ ions, thereby facilitating the specific combination of the Cu2+ ions with the N-CQDs.

Images of the N-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ ions under sunlight and UV light (365 nm) are shown in Fig. 4(b), where significant and distinct colors can be observed for these two samples. As shown in Figs. 4(b-1), under sunlight, the N-CQDs were brown and light blue in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively. After the addition of Cu2+ ions, the color of the N-CQDs changed from bright bluish green to colorless under UV light (365 nm) (Figs. 4(b-2)), which was directly visible to the naked eye. The Cu2+/N-CQDs solution emitted no fluorescence under exposure to UV light (365 nm), thereby indicating that Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching occurred. The quenching of fluorescence by the N-CQDs was clearly visible and this phenomenon was beneficial for the detection of glucose.

Furthermore, the intensity of the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was gradually quenched as the concentration of Cu2+ ions increased. The maximum quenching efficiency was nearly 100% when the concentration of Cu2+ ions exceeded 1500 μM (Fig. 4(c)). The was a strong linear relationship between the quenching efficiency and the concentration of Cu2+ ions in the range from 0 to 70 μM (Fig. 4(d)). The linear equation is: △F/F0 = 0.2245C + 1.5795, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9853, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs, △F is the difference in the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs, and C represents the concentration of Cu2+ ions.

The pH of the solution generally affected the fluorescence quenching rate for the probe, so we investigated the changes in the fluorescence produced by the Cu2+/N-CQDs in aqueous solution at different pH values under weak alkaline conditions. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the pH only had a slight effect on the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs. Similarly, Fig. S4 demonstrates that the fluorescence intensities of the Cu2+/N-CQDs changed little as the pH increased from 7.0 to 12.0, thereby indicating that the Cu2+/N-CQDs in solution were fairly stable in a broad pH region. Thus, they were suitable for the detection of glucose.

The experimental results showed that the coordinated reaction between Cu2+ ions and N-CQDs occurred readily under suitable conditions, where the N-CQDs exhibited clear fluorescence quenching. However, the oxidation reaction with glucose catalyzed by Cu2+/N-CQDs was controlled by the acidity of the solution. Thus, appropriate reaction conditions were necessary in order to perform this reaction successfully. The most suitable temperature for the oxidization reaction with glucose is 80 °C. The fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was still suitable at 80 °C. Thus, we investigated the effect of pH on the performance of the N-CQDs, and control experiments were also conducted under the same conditions.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a-1), the N-CQDs in aqueous solution produced strong fluorescence at 435 nm. After adding a suitable amount (1000 μM) of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was clearly quenched. The pH increased when 1.00 mM glucose was added to the Cu2+/N-CQDs solution and the efficiency of fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs evidently increased. The efficiency of fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs reached a maximum at a pH of 7.5, before decreasing gradually as the pH increased. These results demonstrate that the recovery of fluorescence by the N-CQDs was strongly related to the pH of the solution, where the pH range from 6.0 to 9.0 was most suitable for fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs.

A clear pattern is shown in Figs. 5(a-2) where the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs was maximally recovered by glucose when the mixed solution was adjusted to a pH of 7.0. In addition, strong acid and strong alkaline conditions were not conducive to fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs. The catalytic oxidation of glucose by Cu2+/N-CQDs could not occur in acid solution. The Cu2+ ions could not break away from the surfaces of the N-CQDs and they remained combined with the N-CQDs, so the fluorescence by the N-CQDs was still quenched. The catalytic oxidation of glucose with Cu2+/N-CQDs occurred successfully under alkaline conditions, but superfluous negatively charged hydroxyl groups (OH−) partially coordinated with the positively charged Cu2+ ions to form large amounts of Cu(OH)2 sediment. In addition, more Cu2O was generated via the reaction between Cu(OH)2 and glucose. Due to their strong absorption capacity, the Cu(OH)2 and Cu2O precipitates adsorbed most of the N-CQDs particles, which hindered the production of fluorescence by the N-CQDs. Thus, we determined that the detection of glucose using Cu2+/N-CQDs can be implemented in neutral conditions. The production of Cu2O was confirmed by XRD. As shown in Fig. S5, some strong diffraction peaks appeared at 34.75°, 43.64°, 51.52°, and 75.12°, which were attributed to Cu2O, thereby demonstrating that Cu2O was indeed precipitated in the reaction between the Cu2+/N-CQDs and glucose.

Based on these experimental results, the catalytic oxidation of glucose could be conducted effectively by using Cu2+/N-CQDs at a pH of 7.50 and a temperature of 80 °C. Thus, we next investigated the relationship between the recovered fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs and the concentration of glucose. As shown in Figs. 5(b-1), the N-CQDs in aqueous solution produced strong fluorescence at 435 nm. The fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was obviously quenched when a suitable amount (1000 μM) of Cu2+ was added. Subsequently, we found that the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the glucose concentration, thereby indicating that the fluorescence of the N-CQDs could be recovered. The fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs reached the maximum recovery rate when the concentration of glucose reached 2.0 mM, where it was approximately 80%. The fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs was not recovered completely because of the generation of Cu2O. Thus, when more glucose was added to the Cu2+/N-CQDs solution, more Cu2+ ions were consumed and reduced to yield Cu2O. The Cu2O precipitates adsorbed some of the N-CQDs particles, thereby preventing the full recovery of the fluorescence intensity produced by the N-CQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs was related directly to the concentration of glucose.

The calibration curve determined for the relationship between the glucose concentration and the fluorescence intensity by the N-CQDs is depicted in Figs. 5(b-2). The recovered fluorescence intensity for the N-CQDs when detecting glucose was in a linear range from 0.01 to 1.00 mmol/L and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.455 μmol/L (signal-to-noise ratio is 3). In the linear region, the regression equation is: ΔF/F0 = 21.621C + 49.402, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9972, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs, ΔF is the difference in the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs and Cu2+/N-CQDs, and C represents the concentration of Cu2+ ions. Samples containing three different glucose concentrations of 0.10 mmol/L, 0.50 mmol/L, and 1.00 mmol/L were measured repeatedly three times, and the results indicated that the relative standard deviations were 6.1%, 4.7%, and 7.3%, respectively. Due to the low LOD, samples can be diluted many times before glucose testing. An advantage of diluting the sample is that it can also reduce the concentrations of distractors. In addition, the relatively wide detection range is also suitable for hypoglycemia detection, where the adult fasting blood glucose concentration is lower than 2.8 mmol/L. Thus, the N-CQDs can be employed as probes for detecting glucose at low concentrations.

We compared the analytical performance of the proposed probe with various reported methods and the results are presented in Table 1 , which shows that the N-CQDs probes exhibit higher sensitivity, with a relatively low LOD and a wide linear range response to glucose, thereby facilitating the accurate detection of glucose at low concentrations. This nonenzymatic approach is also more economical compared with the enzymatic method [16] because it takes the advantage of the excellent fluorescence performance of the N-CQDs as fluorescent probes for detecting glucose, thereby avoiding prolonged exposure to a current damaging the CQDs as electrodes in the electrochemical method, as well as providing a more accurate glucose detection approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Cu2+ ion concentration, gluconic acid, and interferents
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of Cu2+ ions is an important parameter during glucose detection using N-CQDs as fluorescent probes in the presence of Cu2+ ions. Therefore, we investigated the effect of the Cu2+ ion concentration on the fluorescence recovery performance of the N-CQDs. For high concentrations (more than 1000 μM) of Cu2+ ions and a fixed amount of N-CQDs, different Cu2+ ion concentrations were tested in combinations with N-CQDs during the detection of glucose. The fluorescence recovery results for the N-CQDs with different Cu2+ ion concentrations are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The N-CQDs in aqueous solution produced strong fluorescence at 435 nm but the fluorescence was obviously quenched after adding a suitable amount (1000 μM) of Cu2+ ions. However, after adding an excessive amount (more than 1000 μM) of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence could not be recovered even in the presence of glucose. Thus, the combination of the N-CQDs with high Cu2+ ion concentrations prevented the successful recognition and detection of glucose, as we expected. It is possible that the number of binding sites available for coordinating with Cu2+ ions was finite on the surfaces of the N-CQDs. Thus, only some of the Cu2+ ions could coordinate with the N-CQDs when a higher Cu2+ ion concentration was added to the N-CQDs solution. Therefore, the majority of the Cu2+ ions did not form bonds with the N-CQDs and they remained in solution. After adding glucose, the Cu2+ ions that originally combined with the N-CQDs were reduced to Cu2O when the Cu2+/N-CQDs catalyzed the oxidation reaction with glucose. The released binding sites on the surfaces of the N-CQDs then recombined with other surplus Cu2+ ions in the solution. As a consequence, the fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs solution remained in a quenched state.

During the detection of glucose, the normal reaction occurs between N-CQDs and Cu2+ ions, and a corresponding amount of gluconic acid is produced because of the oxidation of glucose. Thus, we investigated whether gluconic acid affected the detection of glucose in the Cu2+/N-CQDs system. As shown in Fig. 6(b), gluconic acid produced no fluorescence, and the strong fluorescence produced by the N-CQDs remained almost unchanged after adding different concentrations (0–2.0 mM) of gluconic acid to the N-CQDs solution. These results confirmed that gluconic acid had no effect on the detection of glucose using N-CQDs as fluorescent probes, as well as demonstrating that the fluorescence recovery by the N-CQDs solution was due to the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by Cu2+ ions.

It is important that a probe can distinguish interfering species and the target analyte. In general, glucose coexists with biomolecules and metal ions in the human body. However, the normal physiological level of glucose (3.5–6.1 mM) is much higher than that of other coexisting substances in living organisms, such as ascorbic acid (AA) (0–0.1 mM), uric acid (UA) (0–0.02 mM), and dopamine [11]. Thus, it was necessary to investigate the capacity of the N-CQDs for selectively detecting glucose. The effects of interferents on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs were determined based on the fluorescence recovery, as shown in Fig. 7 . According to Fig. 7(a), after adding Cu2+ ions at 1000 μM, the strong fluorescence emitted by the N-CQDs at 435 nm was quenched dramatically. Subsequently, we added glucose and other interferents that might coexist with glucose (in the blood and urine) to the Cu2+/N-CQDs system under the same conditions. The results showed that the enhancement of the fluorescence by the Cu2+/N-CQDs only occurred after adding glucose, with no increases in fluorescence in the presence of the other interferents, thereby demonstrating that the Cu2+/N-CQDs exhibited high selectivity for glucose. These findings are shown clearly in Fig. 7(b) and Fig. S6. The N-CQDs in aqueous solution produced fluorescence at a high intensity, but the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs decreased by one third after adding Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity recovered to 80% of the original level after adding glucose to the Cu2+/N-CQDs solution. By contrast, the fluorescence intensity produced by the Cu2+/N-CQDs solution after adding interferents was almost the same as that without them. It was interesting that the addition of dopamine made the fluorescence intensity decrease further and this could slightly affect the results obtained in glucose testing. However, the dopamine content of the human body is normally low. Only a small amount of dopamine is secreted when the brain is stimulated and its concentration decreases and even disappears as it is metabolized [49]. These findings suggest that interferents did not change the fluorescence intensity of the Cu2+/N-CQDs. Therefore, using the N-CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions may be advantageous for identifying glucose without using glucose oxidase. Similar to the enzymatic glucose detection approach, this method is specific only to glucose. The Cu2+/N-CQDs complex only catalyzes the oxidation of glucose and it does not interact with other interferents as a nonenzymatic probe. Our results demonstrate that the proposed method is highly selective for distinguishing glucose from other biomolecules and inorganic ions.

Glucose detection methods increasingly use tears, sweat, and urine to replace blood glucose testing. These methods are effective at alleviating the pain associated with collecting blood from patients. In order to demonstrate the feasibility of using N-CQDs for the actual detection of glucose, we analyzed real urine samples. The results obtained after adding different concentrations of glucose (0 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, and 1.0 mM) to diluted urine under identical experimental conditions are shown in Table 2 . Satisfactory recoveries were obtained for the three tested samples, i.e., 99.10%, 93.88%, and 102.61%. These results suggest that our method can be applied in practice to assay glucose in real urine samples, with good accuracy and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of glucose detection using Cu2+/N-CQDs as fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The glucose detection process is illustrated schematically in Fig. 8 . It is well known that the fluorescent emissions from most CQDs are due to the radiative recombination of excitons trapped by surface defects, which can be quenched by metal ions via nonradiative electron transfer between CQDs and metal ions [50]. Abundant functional groups such as COOH, OH, and NH2 were obtained on the surfaces of the N-CQDs due to the preparation process employed. After adding Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity was reduced for the N-CQDs in aqueous solution. In the Cu2+/N-CQDs complex, the doped nitrogen atoms on the surfaces of the N-CQDs activated the neighboring sp2 carbon atoms to enhance the catalytic capability. In addition, the N atoms had a high thermodynamic affinity and they integrated rapidly with the Cu2+ ions, thereby enhancing the affinity in the coordination with Cu2+ ions to form Cu2+/N-CQDs complexes, and facilitating charge transfers from the excited state N-CQDs to the Cu2+ ions.

In previously reported glucose detection methods, the metal particles combined with CQDs are usually etched by H2O2, which is generated via the oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase, thereby enhancing the fluorescence in response to the glucose concentration [12,14]. However, the use of an enzyme is expensive and the operating conditions are restrictive [51]. To overcome these disadvantages, we developed a Cu2+/N-CQDs complex to replace glucose oxidase during the catalytic oxidation of glucose in weakly alkaline conditions. In a weakly acidic solution, the NH2 groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs exist in molecular form, and they provide lone pair electrons to form coordination bonds with Cu2+ ions. As the pH of the solution gradually increases, the OH− ions increasingly react with Cu2+ ions via a competitive process with NH2. The negatively charged OH− ions readily react with the positively charged Cu2+ ions to form a N-CQDs-Cu(OH) complex, which is similar to Cu(OH)2 in form, and it can catalyze the oxidation reaction with glucose. Subsequently, the glucose enters the reaction system and it is oxidized to gluconic acid by the N-CQDs-Cu(OH) complex. The Cu2+ ions then leave the surfaces of the N-CQDs because they are reduced to Cu2O. Finally, the fluorescence is recovered by the N-CQDs. The fluorescence recovery rate of the N-CQDs is related to the amount of added Cu2+ ions. Therefore, the fluorescence recovery rate increases gradually as the amount of Cu2+ ions combined with the N-CQDs decreases. After the departure of all the Cu2+ ions, the maximum recovery of the fluorescence emission occurs for the N-CQDs. Thus, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs can be recovered via the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by Cu2+ ions even without using glucose oxidase.

In this study, we developed a method for nonenzymatic glucose detection based on Cu2+ catalytic oxidation on N-CQDs. This is a novel method for detecting glucose using a Cu2+/N-CQDs nanocomplex combined with fluorescence analysis under nonenzymatic conditions. The size distribution of the spherical N-CQDs particles obtained by microwave hydrothermal synthesis was mainly in the range of 1.0–2.0 nm around 1.4 nm. A large number of functional groups were distributed on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, including OH, COOH, and NH2. The chemical properties of the N-CQDs were fairly stable under different conditions (pH value, temperature, and ionic strength). In the presence of Cu2+ ions, due to electron transfer, the bluish green light emitted by the N-CQDs under excitation by UV light (365 nm) was quenched by the Cu2+ ions. Furthermore, Cu2+/N-CQDs were formed to detect glucose without using glucose oxidase. When the oxidation reaction with glucose catalyzed by Cu2+/N-CQDs was conducted at 80 °C in a weak alkaline solution (pH = 7.50), the Cu2+ ions left the surfaces of the N-CQDs and the maximal fluorescence recovery efficiency was obtained for the N-CQDs. Under the optimal conditions, we determined a linear relationship in the range of 0.01–1.00 mmol/L for the glucose concentration with respect to the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs and the LOD was 0.455 μmol/L. Our results demonstrated that the activity of the Cu2+/N-CQDs was remarkable during the catalytic oxidation of glucose in the absence of glucose oxidase, thereby providing an effective fluorescence method for the nonenzymatic detection of glucose. Importantly, coexisting biomolecules and inorganic ions did not interfere with the detection of glucose in the proposed method. We anticipate that this green, low cost, and sensitive method can be employed for detecting glucose in industrial applications and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (21371084, 31570154) and Major Science and Technology Project of Water Pollution Control and Management in China (2015ZX07202-012). We also thank our colleagues and other students for participating in this study.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2018.08.025.
